replies.title: Re: Etheric Cooking and House Plants
replies.content:
Just curious, JMG, do you and Sara like house plants?

To the OP: might try growing a lemon tree from a (grocery store lemon) seed. It's surprisingly easy. Soak the lemon seeds in water for a couple of hours, gently peel off the outer husks, and put them in a plastic ziplock bag for about a week with a wet paper towel inside. They will sprout and then you will transfer them to small pots with good drainage. I started growing lemon trees from seed as part of my Druid studies a few years ago and one of them is rather large, approximately 18" tall. Citrus trees bring solar energy into your space.

I also find that houseplants like to be "watered" with ice. Meaning, you put ice cubes of them instead of watering them. I ice "water" my snake plants and pothos. Healthy, well maintained houseplants bring good energy into the home. Keep in mind almost all plants love a haircut, meaning that pruning and dividing them does not hurt them. And don't over-water. Less is usually more.

replies.title: Re: Book
replies.content:
I just did a World Catalogue search on Watkins' "The Ley Hunter's Manual," and discovered that the first edition was published in 1927. Watkins' more detailed book on the same subject, "The Old Straight Track," was published in 1925.

Any book published in 1925 or earlier entered the public domain in the USA as of January 1, 2021, so as of that date anyone is free to scan "The Old Straight Track" and put it up on archive.org or some similar site. In fact, that's already been done. it's available on archive.org, scanned and put up there by the Wellcome Library in London.

For "The Ley Hunter's Manual," however, one would need to wait until January 1, 2023, to scan it and put it up on the web legally.

That said, a web search did turn up several places where one might download "The Ley Hunter's Manual" if one weren't inclined to be over-scrupulous. The only such sites that I found are notorious for requiring one to set up an account with personal information before downloading anything. Myself, I wouldn't care to deal with any site that wanted that much personal information. (Your mileage may vary, of course.)

comments.title: Bible as Occult text
comments.content:
Sir,
What do you think of Manly P Hall’s “How to understand your bible?” Also do you or the commentariat have a recommended version? I know that they are often arranged with a particular non-occult theology in mind. Since I know little Greek and zero Hebrew I’d like an edition that doesn’t put up barriers to interpretation since I can’t diy.

— Indigo Refulgent Toad

replies.title: Re: Bible as Occult text
replies.content:
1) Hall had his own take on the Bible; it's worth reading, though he'd be the first person to tell you not to accept it on faith.

2) Every translation presupposes an interpretation -- you literally can't translate something out of an ancient language without interpreting it, because Hebrew and Greek both have a very small vocabulary by the standards of modern languages, so you have to decide which of a dozen different words you're going to use to translate any given word in those languages. I tend to use the King James version despite its inaccuracies, because the language is beautiful and the biases are very obvious, thus easy to dodge.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Having gone through the same thing myself: read lots of books and get creative.

Since starting ritual magic, my time with videogames has gone down from hours a day to a couple hours a week. I fill my time now with paper books and audio books I can listen to while going on nature walks or working out. I still watch youtube, but mostly for academic religious channels like Esoterica, livestreams from practitioners like Damien Echols, and the Astrology Podcast when I'm doing zodiacal and planetary invocations.

Obviously occult books to support your magical development are great, but inspiring fiction to improve the quality of your imagination can be just as helpful. If you like comics, I highly recommend Alan Moore's Promethea, which mixes superhero storytelling with occult philosophy. JMG's own Weird of Hali series also has some delightfully practical magic tips sprinkled in.

I suggest cutting out social media as soon as you can. Once I started becoming sensitive to my own energy field, I realized social media was cutting holes in my attention and ability to concentrate. Online forums for group study are a great alternative and there are a bunch on discord.

The big idea is that you're transitioning your life from relying on other people's imagination (movies, games), to your own imagination. It's comparable to moving out of your parents' house, and it's okay to feel a little bit lost at first. Creative writing, art, anything else that makes you more interesting to yourself is worth your time.

On the plus side, if you work on your social skills, this can make you more interesting to other people too. It's better to be a source of meaning and interest for the world and not just a consumer.

I see more and more books appearing attempting to connect the use of psychoactive substances with any ancient spiritual tradition. replies.title: Re: Psychedelics and the occult
replies.content:
My thoughts amount to "Oh, goodie, here we go again." That's one of those perennial bad ideas, like masturbation as performance art, that get recycled every thirty years or so. The 1960s and 1990s both saw a bumper crop of literature along those lines.

Traditional occultism will survive just fine, fortunately. What will happen is that a bunch of people will go binge on the latest fashionable drugs under the mistaken notion that they're doing something spiritual, a fair number of them will fry their nervous systems or otherwise demonstrate why that notion is a bad idea, and said notion will be shelved until the 2040s. It's been happening since the 19th century -- there were people back then who insisted that chloroform, ether, and opium were the keys to expanded consciousness, blah blah blah.

This question is long. It arises from two parallel issues, and it’s difficult for me to formulate it. Please be patient.

I have been a private devotee of the Greek pantheon for 3 years. Maybe more, I’m not sure anymore.

As at home as I feel with these deities, my spiritual practice is solitary, and with the lockdown, and loss of Freemason lodge meetings (among other losses), the social isolation has been getting to me. My divorce process is slow, but at least I’m on amicable terms with my former-wife-to-be, and I’m seeing my daughters readily and regularly, with our ups and downs, though at least the ‘ups’ outweigh the ‘downs’, and I am thankful for that. My isolation is making me feel desperate. My first issue is my private religion: My solitary devotion to the Greek deities feels unsatisfactory because of the solitude, and I believe they are understanding. Your posting some months back on henotheism was a real shot in the arm to look at other options; my nominal Jewishness (by conversion) does not work because the Jewish community I belong to is high-end PMC, and there is no real sympathy towards ‘deplorables’; they are into wokesterism. Even though I’m PMC myself, I’ve never felt like I belonged. My father, who was an electrical engineer, never emotionally left his depression era hardship. My mother, for all her expensive tastes and pretensions, never really stopped being the poor daughter of an uneducated cowboy/logger and his uneducated Puerto Rican camp-cook wife. And they brought me up with an iron-fist ‘deplorables’ shame-based upbringing.

So with the failure of my Jewish marriage, my alien Jewish community, and the Jewish G-d’s throne looking all too empty, I’m looking again at Christianity. Off the git-go, Catholicism is off limits because of the dysfunctional system of clergy/unaccountability/abuse. Protestantism is out because it feels like ideology-lite (or heavy) in thin religious drag. So I’m looking at Orthodox Christianity, and regularly attending services, including some vespers, at two Orthodox Churches. Again, the Covid restrictions severely limit social activity/events. But I’m reading some Orthodox literature, and am struck at how extreme it is. For example, a proper Christian has no Will!. Our Wills are so corrupted by sin as to be irredeemable, that all we can do is surrender them, and let God take over! On the question of marriage and courtship, the literature discusses being a “Eunuch for Christ”, citing Jesus himself in Matthew 19, and/or St Paul’s writings somewhere. "Eunuch"! I found this nauseating. I read some traditional Catholic literature, and came across this same garbage, and when I discussed this with a friend of mine who was reared Protestant (now Jewish), she replied that her upbringing emphasized the same thing, and that was why she left! I can’t join a religion that really demands this of me. So I thought I’d track down some classic, unassailable Christian literature with an agreed-upon interpretation, to really get a handle on these issues. My reading list was to include Pilgrim’s Progress, Paradise Lost, On the Imitation of Christ, and Dante. My favorite used bookstore was out of stock on these, and as the proprietor had searched so diligently for me, I felt bad not buying something. So I turned around to glance at the shelves behind me, my eye immediately caught a title by Dion Fortune: The Goat Foot God. So I bought it, and you can imagine the proprietor had quite the guffaw. Well, I just finished it, and it certainly did nothing to diminish my impression of the Jewish/Christian god as being analogous to an abusive husband who keeps his wife locked in the house and isolated from all society. I’m pretty perturbed by The Goat Foot God, basically because the main character, Hush Paston, resembles me too closely for comfort. I’m perturbed enough that I feel a need to act on this novel somehow, but I’m not sure what to do. I certainly don’t resemble Hugh in his wealth!

The second issue I have been coping with is Asperger’s Syndrome, and recently, I’ve had several painful insights where I was able to see myself as others see me, and I’ve extending this insight retrospectively in quite a few anguished recollections. These insights occurred during my reading of The Goat Foot God. I have, until now, been so fixated on my ‘intelligence’ (as conventionally understood) as the foundation for my sense of self worth, that I did not adequately perceive or appreciate my social and interpersonal incompetence, which is really rather profound. Now that I ‘see’ it, I’m feeling dejected to use no stronger word. My interpersonal shortcomings arising from my Aspergers have been so detrimental to my interpersonal relations, that it’s a wonder I’m alive, let alone not on the street. It is a major factor behind my failed marriage, and poor relationship with my daughters. I cope with AS the way you would cope with a cat trying to escape out the door: Whenever you open the open the door (read “mouth”), you watch the cat and make sure it doesn’t escape. I can do that pretty reliably when I’m, say, with a patient, but if I’m distracted, pressured, emotional or dealing with an emotional topic, my vigilance fails me and the 'cat'(inappropriate remark) escapes. And now that I can see it at least somewhat from the perspective of others, I’m frankly depressed by it.

So I have these two parallel issues, i) finding a ritual-based religious/spiritual home that isn’t crazy/bizarre, and whether/how to respond to The Goat Foot God in particular; and ii) coping with Asperger’s, I will be seeking out a new counselor now that I have more grasp of it, but I doubt that will be enough.

(I noticed in The Goat Foot God how Hughs friend Jelkes had applied The Method of St. Ignatius of Loyola to Iamblicus’s Mysteries. I can’t help but think Dion Fortune herself esteemed such a study, though it seems like too arduous an undertaking for me now. I assume that "Pan" is identical to Dionysus.)

I do keep daily LRP and MP exercises, and daily devotions to the Hellenistic gods, and now, to Mary and Jesus.

So, does my account call to mind an occult approach to these issues?

If you're partial to the Greek pantheon, but you're interested in Christianity, might I suggest looking at the Greek Orthodox Church? They don't worship the old gods, but are proud of their Classical heritage and regularly name their kids Zeus, Ares, Heracles, and the like.

It sounds to me like whatever you've read about Orthodoxy is based on the severe Russian ascetic literature. This stuff should be avoided like the plague by a newcomer, because it's written by and for monks, and for those who want a pure, ascetic lifestyle. That whole "eunuchs" thing is only trotted out in this kind of literature, for people following a totally celibate life - otherwise, believe me, you never hear it mentioned. (Orthodoxy, like other forms of Christianity, promote chastity for the unmarried and faithfulness in marriage.) Also, the bit about giving up one's will: in the ascetic literature, one gives up his will to his starets (spiritual elder) in a relationship not unlike the guru and the chela. It only works if one lives in a monastery or other closed community. This kind of extreme ascetic thinking is only for individuals specifically called to it, and definitely not for normal people living in "the world" that is, everyday social reality.

The books generally recommended to beginners are those by Bishop Kallistos Ware (or Timothy Ware), The Orthodox Church and The Orthodox Way. The latter is about the belief system, and the former contains a lot of history as well.

Orthodoxy is not a DIY religion, that you can understand by reading about it. You need personal guidance through the complexity of practice and vocabulary. I suggest talking to a few parish priests - they are mostly competent, well-educated, and if they've been doing it very long, experienced in how to fit an ancient faith into a modern world that is mostly unfriendly toward it. They are happy to help sort out the thinking of a newcomer or even someone who is just curious. They can also recommend more helpful books, of which there are many, but again you need someone to guide you through it.

You may, in fact, discover that Orthodoxy is not for you - not everyone is comfortable with it, especially those raised in the West - but you may find some useful spiritual practices there to guide you toward wherever your calling turns out to be. The Prayer of the Heart, or the "Jesus Prayer" is quite popular among non-Orthodox, and many find that some adaptation of the fasting practices to be beneficial.

And although it's frowned on by the clergy, what the Russians call dvoyeveriye, "double faith", in which the old gods are given a place within a particular person's Orthodox practices, is hardly unknown. This happens particularly in Russia, where after the lifting of Bolshevik oppression, both the Church and Slavic forms of paganism are on the rise, and sometimes mix together in very odd ways. :)

Wherever your path takes you, I wish you luck and blessings, as, I'm sure do most of the rest of us here.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The commenter above has a good response: stay away from the monastic literature unless you are called to the monastic life, or your confessor/spiritual advisor recommends it. As a catechumen, we were warned about this. At my first Orthodox church, the priest had actually banned certain literature from the church library, because he had got tired of having to sort people out after they'd read it and got themselves all in a tangle.

Ware's books are good clear texts (if a bit dense) for understanding Orthodoxy. I'd also recommend Frederica Matthews-Green's "Welcome to the Orthodox Church" as a good user-friendly starting point that covers all the basics. It's an easy read, and her approach doesn't assume you already know anything about Orthodoxy.

Prayer ropes have been mentioned: I'll again pass on the advice of my catechist. Those are best used as part of a regimen assigned by your priest or spiritual advisor. Kind of like your doctor prescribing medication. There's a lot of potential benefit there, maybe also some hazards. Ask your spiritual doctor which prayer rule is right for you! ;)

Tempting as it is for us aspie data-centric, obsessive-research, know-everything-before-you-invest types (and I am absolutely one of them), Orthodoxy is not a thing you can read up on, work out all the details in your head, and have a complete comprehension of without participating in it. I'm not at all sure it is possible to *ever* have complete comprehension, and I would not have it any other way: I grew up Calvinist, and the tendency there is to dissect every single aspect of God and theology, pin them all to the tray with little numbered labels, and in this way keep God at a safe distance, where you feel like you're in control. Orthodoxy is the worship of God, who is not fully comprehensible to us. We do the best we can --there are many many things (perhaps most things) that can only be understood by *doing*-- it is a process, a gradual unfolding. There are so many things in Orthodoxy, big and small, that made no sense when I read about them. Having participated in them for years now, some of them I kind of get. Not everything is amenable to a verbal or literary understanding.

I don't know if it's the right place for you to end up, but I hope you'll give it a fair shake. And the best way to do that is to go to the services and see. It's deeply frustrating that the COVID restrictions inhibit things so much right now.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Sphere of Protection didn't start out as a Druid ritual. It was created by the late John Gilbert, on the basis of an earlier ritual created by Juliet Ashley, and it was used by the whole set of esoteric orders Ashley once headed -- the Universal Gnostic Church, the Order of Spiritual Alchemy, the Order of Modern Essenes, the Holy Order of the Golden Dawn, and the Ancient Order of Druids in America. So you can very easily pursue Cabalistic magic while practicing the SoP.

On the other hand, the classic Golden Dawn worked extensively with Egyptian gods, and it would be easy to convert the LBRP to work with the Egyptian pantheon -- here's your template. So you can go either way.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That would be complex, because you can't just plop the SoP into the standard Golden Dawn magical system -- it doesn't do the same things as the LBRP, and it doesn't work with quite the same energies. You'd need to construct a system of Cabalistic magic more or less parallel to the system in The Druid Magic Handbook, building on the SoP in much the same way. That wouldn't be especially difficult, but it would take some work.

comments.title: Stonehenge
comments.content:
The photo for this post reminded me of a question/comment I had - a friend's relatives live fairly near to Stonehenge. Since the 1980s, there have been proposals to *do something* about the nearby A303 road, which is always horribly congested (it goes from a major 4-lane road to a 2-lane road next to Stonehenge for a few miles, so all the traffic backs up). All of these proposals have failed, at various stages of progress. This isn't terribly unusual for the UK, but I do wonder if there isn't something more magical about it. There is a fairly prominent druid nearby who names himself Arthur Pendragon (you may even have met him!) who is vehemently against all road development near the site and I do wonder if he and his other druidic friends have done some kind of working around it to permanently frustrate further road building.

The latest plan to technically be approved is a tunnel under the site, then to build an elevated roadway across the adjacent valley using the excavated soil. Based on how many archaeological sites can be found even when digging up a garden, and the requirement for construction to halt if an archaeological site is uncovered, I have a fairly low confidence in how well this plan will fare...

Stonehenge is, interestingly, also sited on a type of rock known as phosphatic chalk. This type of chalk is actually mildly radioactive - enough to show up on a Geiger counter, but not enough to give you radiation sickness or be otherwise particularly obvious. (Unless, of course, you crush it into a fine dust, when, for example, digging a mile-long tunnel...) Other locations in Europe where phosphatic chalk is present are also sites of ancient religious significance, so I do wonder what the relationship between radiation and the other planes is... It would also explain why the ancient builders bothered to lug however many tonnes of rock all the way from Wales!

replies.title: Re: Stonehenge
replies.content: I've never met Arthur Pendragon but of course I'm familiar with his work. I suspect you're right and no such road will be built. As for the phosphatic chalk, one of the lost secrets of the old megalithic mysteries is precisely how different kinds of subsoil and stone affect human spiritual experience. It's a question worth much meditation and research.

replies.title: Re: Stonehenge
replies.content:
Thanks, I agree that it would be amazing to know the real impact of "place" in terms of all the local environmental factors (its own Terroir, or balance of the four elements, if you will) on the more subtle levels. Maybe some day that knowledge will be re-learned all over again.

For what it's worth, I personally think that, in much the same way that a small amount of stress can invigorate the immune and nervous systems, but a large amount will overload it, a small amount of background radiation can enliven the more subtle bodies and senses. In fact, as another random anecdote, I had an ex-girlfriend who worked in a mothballed nuclear facility for a period. Only one other person worked there with her, in the same office. She told me how they heard no end of weird noises in the facility, and strangeness such as moved tools, lights not working, all the usual haunting stuff. I always wondered if the many years of radiation had effectively weakened the walls between the planes.

replies.title: Re: Neopagans and their magic
replies.content:
In Denning and Phillips' book on creative visualization, they make exactly this point. The book was written to cash in on what was then a popular New Age practice; but being written by experienced occultists, it is more grounded than other books published back then. (Not a high bar to clear, I grant you!) In their instructions for the visualization session, they emphasize invoking the Divine Presence in the usual Western-occult way of visualizing the Kether center above the head, aspiring the "the greatest good one is capable of conceiving", and drawing down the energy into one's personal sphere. After the visualization is complete, the image is filled with the Divine energy before being sent on its way.

The authors said that visualizations done without this connection to the Divine will work, but they amount to what the traditional literature calls "glamour": superficial and temporary results that fall apart once the natural energy put into them by the practitioner is depleted. The consequences vary, of course, but the results of success followed quickly by loss isn't pleasant. Back in the day, I saw many New Age creative visualizers fall into this trap, and when the "glamour" goes away, it tends to take a good chunk of one's ordinary life with it. The usual explanation was that "you're not visualizing clearly enough!", and some who didn't straighten out right away would get on a kind of treadmill of gain and loss until they finally gave up.

Frater T.O.S. (Taupe Orthogonal Raccoon)

replies.content:

These books were written in a lightweight style, as compared to D&P's more serious work, and "serious occultists" often see them, as I hinted above, as ways of making money off gullible people. My take on it is that they were attempts to inject some serious occult thinking into popular practices. (And as you yourself are no doubt aware, there's nothing wrong with a writer making money on little side projects! :) ) Some of the prose hasn't aged well, but these books are much more readable than similar books on the subject published at about the same time, and they give magical techniques that just about anyone can use with a little practice.

comments.title: "Inner Contacts"
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

There is a magical group or two that I've encountered that refers to having "inner contacts" which initiates might eventually establish some rapport with, ostensibly to assist them in their magical development. replies.title: Re: "Inner Contacts"
replies.content:
Inner plane contacts are something you pick up if you do a lot of the kind of imaginal work that the Golden Dawn system of magic does. What happens is that some of the imaginal beings you encounter offer to assist you, for reasons of their own. This isn't trance mediumship -- you don't hand over your body to them; you simply talk with them in that peculiar state of vivid imagination that children with imaginary friends know so well -- and they quite often pass on remarkably detailed and effective magical and occult teachings.

Some of them present themselves as the souls of people who lived in the past; others present themselves with other identities. Are they who they claim to be? I have no idea -- and yes, I work with several of these beings. Can working with them be a useful and valuable experience? Yes. That being the case, if you've gotten some experience with Pathworkings and the like, you might consider using an image from one of their books as a gateway, go in, and ask if the occultist would like to talk. Always banish before and after.

comments.title: Demonic Activity During Sleep Paralysis.
comments.content:
Hello JMG and the commentariat

I had a pretty intense experience the other night that I've been trying to make sense of.

Sleep paralysis and sensing shadowy figures (and other experiences) are not unexpected occurrences for me. Recently however, something different happened: It started out as a standard vivid dream. At some point I was looking out the window in a dream-geometry version of my bedroom, when I had the feeling of inviting something else in, and then the next moment I was laying in bed (still in the dream room) paralyzed while a demonic figure was over me having sex with me (don't know if this figures into anything but I'm a gay man, and if I'm coding the experience onto masculine / feminine it was a masculine energy over my more feminine energy at the time.) The thing that is unsettling in hindsight is the feeling of inviting it in and experiencing a sort of sexy-vampire thrill.

I then began floating out of my body and around the room, still unable to move, but somewhat able to control where I was going. This sort of out of body experience has only happened once to me before, though I’ve seen the link between sleep paralysis and OBE before (and this time I was still in the dream version of my room rather than an actual room.)

This prompted me to get your book on Monsters (it was on my list for a while and this seemed to push it up the priority list!) and check out the demon chapter. It was really informative, but I wanted to know how exactly to think of demonic obsession, vs experiencing demonic activity, vs garden variety mental illness?

I mostly tend to look at my own issues through the lens of mental illness, and take steps to work on myself with a therapist and my own solo strategies. I also direct many of my daily discursive meditation and simple one-card daily tarot readings to gain insight into my own mental health challenges.

In your book however (and elsewhere in your blog), the characterization of demonic activity as “robotic thought patterns” that accompany an obsession seemed to resonate with me.

My meditation on this so far seems to be leading me towards a similar treatment whether I’m being influenced by an external entity, or if I’m just battling my own internal demons. Looked at symbolically the dream could be seen as me inviting in my vices in a passive way, and enjoying them on a degraded level. If there is an external entity, my passivity can be seen as providing this entity a connection to me. Synchronistically I’m actually up to the chapter in cosdoc on the law of action and reaction so will be reading about this more fully, but skipping ahead a bit in your posts I saw some hints that it will be helpful in understanding how to work on slipping into these degraded states (based on what I’ve read so far, it reminds me of the “short circuit”: where you bypass moving up to higher levels and operate on a lower plane.)

One other quick red flag I noticed is I usually am able to tie some of the unproductive ruts my mind gets in with tension in my lower neck. Again, this can be described as trauma living within my body, but your book explicitly mentioned the base of the neck as an entry point...

I guess my question is: I perceive what’s happening as a gradation. I succumb to negative thoughts / potentially an external entity sometimes, other times I’m operating on a more enlightened level. If I’m not careful I could follow those thoughts / desires to some pretty bad places, or I could continue trying to fight against them, or integrate them into my life in a healthier way.

Obsession seems to be more of an all or nothing thing however, you’re either linked to an entity or not. Assuming this isn’t just mental illness, is it possible something just has an “interest” in me, and is just taking advantage of moments of weakness, or might I be in the earlier stages of something more serious?

replies.title: Re: Demonic Activity During Sleep Paralysis.
replies.content: It sounds to me as though it's a common or garden variety experience, not necessarily anything serious. If you don't currently practice a daily banishing ritual, however, you might consider taking one up.

replies.title: Re: Demonic Activity During Sleep Paralysis.
replies.content:
I have been meaning to make a post about geometric patterns used as natural magic to avoid hypnogogic interference and your question spurred me into action. Hope this helps:

https://kimberlysteele.dreamwidth.org/26916.html

Long story short is that geometric patterns work as demon traps -- they draw in the entities who would have their way with you and lock them in an unending pattern. The way I discovered this bit of natural magic was partly by reading one of the many humorous observations in Josephine McCarthy's The Exorcist's Handbook as well as my own meditations about Islamic art and its avoidance of patterns with human or animal faces in them. One of the reasons ancient churches and temples are so full of symmetrical, repeating, geometric designs is because they fascinate and deter certain entities, and demons are among those entities. Angels and gods, however, aren't trapped by geometric designs because angels and gods aren't prone in that specific sense.

comments.title: Three Newbie Questions
comments.content:
Dear JMG (and community),

I am a longtime reader of your other/former blogs, but am relatively new here (first comment). I don't have any previous training or formal experience in working with the divine in my life. Over the years I have read broadly in an effort to find a tradition that seemed to dovetail well with my own life experience and interests, and I feel that in Druidry I have found something to really dig into and apply myself at. Many thanks to you and the community for providing such a wonderful resource for people like me.

I have purchased your Druidry Handbook, Druid Magic Handbook and Secret of the Temple and am working through them. As to practice, I have started meditating (using primarily your series of posts on this blog), and I have been reading through your instructions on the SoP in your DMH, which I hope to start soon.

My first question relates to the gesturing in the SoP, and specifically raising ones arms above ones head to join the hands. I am very tall, and live in a basement apartment with very low ceilings. The most headroom I can find in this apartment is five inches, which significantly limits how high I can raise my arms, and the height at which I can join my hands. I would normally be inclined to just go out to the backyard in the early morning to do my SoP, but I live in a cold part of Canada. I can imagine that this might not be an obstacle for someone experienced with the practice, but I suspect the weather might interfere with me maintaining the proper focus as a beginner (though perhaps no more so than the ceiling issue would). Once spring comes I will have more options, but I'd prefer to get started on the SoP sooner than that. Do you have any suggestions for how I might start practising a SoP given my environmental constraints? Having read many of the very interesting questions and erudite comments on this blog, I feel a little silly asking such a mundane question, but your answer will be very helpful to me.

My second question relates to my beloved dog Luna of almost thirteen years, whom I put to rest three weeks ago after her cancer became too much for her. I gather from reading your comments that her soul's passage is likely to last roughly 40 days. I have very little experience with praying, but have been doing my best to help Luna along. I have been sitting twice daily in more structured sessions, praying aloud and silently to God, expressing gratitude for bringing Luna and I together, and asking God to guide and protect Luna's soul on its journey. I plan to do this for 40 - 60 days. I have also been talking to Luna (aloud or silently) throughout the day, especially when I walk or hike in the woods we used to frequent together. In addition to the efforts at verbal communication, I have been forming images of her in my mind, and allowing to come forward all the positive emotions that those images elicit in me. I'm not sure what the best way is to reach her and try to express love and reassure her (or if I even can). I would be enormously grateful for any guidance you can provide me in this regard.

Finally, I will be creating an urn for Luna's remains. I won't be able to use wood from the forests we hiked together (though the wood will almost certainly be reasonably local), but I will be incorporating some stones from the creeks and rivers we swam and paddled together. I won't be able to gather these stones until the final snow and ice has melted in May, so I have some time ahead of me to plan this out properly. I use the golden ratio in many of my woodworking designs, but wondered if there are any other particular design considerations that would be appropriate for an urn that will be the resting place for the remains of a loved one.

My thanks in advance, and my gratitude to you for fostering such a wonderful community.

replies.title: Re: Three Newbie Questions
replies.content:
1) Bring the fingertips together just above your forehead and draw the hands down to your forehead from there. The movement is symbolic, thus permits a lot of flexibility.

2) Please accept my condolences! What you're doing is in fact the best thing to do.

3) The design considerations that appeal most to you, and do the best job of expressing what you feel. There's no universal rule.

replies.content:
What our host said: TV is an addictive machine, and a fairly powerful one at that. The addictive effects are achieved (it seems to me) by specific ways in which the intensity of sound and of light are made to vary in patterned ways during a program. The initial economic advantage seems to have been to TV advertisers, but soon, no doubt, the networks figured out how to use the same addiction deliberately to boost their ratings.

I suppose this technique developed by trial and error at first, but eventually clever people began to theorize about it. A former student of mine at my university went to work for an on-line gaming company after graduation. Some years later our paths crossed and we talked a little about this very subject. He happened to mention to me that his company and others like it held regular scholarly conferences, complete with presentions of research papers, to advance their control of these addictive processes. I asked where this research was published and how I might see at least some of these papers. He replied that the papers were never openly published, and that they weren't available even to most employees of the company, but only to a trusted inner core--rather like classified research for the government.

It seems to me that the same sort of thing is starting to happen now in the world of online newsfeeds and similar sites, though some other mechanism than patterned changes in the rhythms of light and sound seems to be building addiction here. (I never have the sound on when I am online.)

I don't like being subjected to addictive pressure; if it gets much stronger, I will probably stay offline almost completely. (I stopped watching TV about 35 years ago when it got to be too addictive.) I reckon I have maybe five more years of regular screen activity online before it becomes too much.

comments.title: Blake
comments.content:
Greetings JMG,

(1) I've noticed a tendency among spiritual types to over-attribute what appear to be mundane events (e.g. say, thinking a license plate number they see constitutes a personal message from the Universe) with events which may be rightfully attributable to higher planes. Now, there are cases which are more easy to distinguish, say, the psychological projections of a mentally ill person versus a case of premonition which the subject could not have possibly known through mundane means, or perhaps an extraordinary synchronicity. The problem is there is often a wide gray-area in between the two classes of event, for example, a person has leaves a singularly negative impression upon you and you are consistently bothered by the thought of the person. How could one distinguish between psychic interference and mere psychological projection in such a case? Similarly, how can one distinguish between a 'mere' coincidence and a bona fide synchronicity? Generally: Are there any reliable ways to differentiate between an 'authentic, out-there' higher-plane event and a mundane one?

(2) Could the words and concepts associated with Christianity (or for that matter, any other spiritual system) carry a negative vibe received from its often ignorant or persecuting adherents, past and present? It doesn't personally bother me much in practice, but I am aware of the atrocities committed in the name of Christianity, as well as the often oppressive characteristics attributed to their God, and am concerned about taking on those traits in my practice.

(3) I've been practicing eating a vegetarian diet, with the occasional exception of fish/seafood, for a several years and I feel like it makes me more spacey or aloof, since I'm lacking the grounding energies of meat. Is there a vegetarian-friendly means to counteract this?

Thank you kindly.

replies.title: Re: Blake
replies.content:
1) It's not easy. Practice, experience, and the habit of systematically checking apparent inspirations and messages against what actually happens in the world are essential.

2) Yes. Every religious and spiritual tradition carries the burden of its history. In the terms I use, there's a collective karma that goes with every tradition, and if you become part of that tradition, you get your share of the karma.

3) No. That's why ceremonial mages are advised not to follow a vegetarian diet; over and above the possible material effects (some people don't process vegetable protein well, for example), the psychological effects don't combine well with magic. You might want to consider bringing small amounts of humanely and sustainably raised meat into your diet.

replies.title: Re: Blake
replies.content: 2) Do you know what the collective karma of Druidry and/or the Golden Dawn looks like?

replies.title: Re: Blake
replies.content: Yes, of course. Druidry's karma is pretty mild, and mostly leads toward eccentricity. The Golden Dawn has a serious problem with ego inflation among its adepts, which is made worse by the structure of the classic rituals (one reason why Dion Fortune among others dropped those and did something different); it's common for GD temples to end up either breaking apart due to internal politics or getting into long and frankly silly wars with other temples. There are ways to prevent that, and I think there's good reason to hope for better things in times to come, but if you get into the GD it's something you need to watch out for.

replies.title: Re: Blake
replies.content: For someone who's picking up classic Golden Dawn work, do you have advice for avoiding getting caught up with ego inflation? (I'm starting work with LRM, and using the LBRP, Middle Pillar, and Rose Cross from Circles of Power)

replies.title: Re: Blake
replies.content: Daily meditation is essential, as it keeps your mind clear. Also, when you get to the point of working with planetary energies, don't fixate on solar energy -- that way lies a ballooning ego. Work with all the planets, and with the elements as well; and make regular use of the traditional prayers given in GD rituals, as those will help keep you from losing track of the difference between yourself and the divine.

replies.title: Re: Blake
replies.content: 3)I would suppose that, like anything else, it's a double-edged sword. I could say for example that while vegetarianism has made lesser my ability to effectively connect with situations and people on the instinctual, animal level, it has by the same token provided me a certain degree of freedom from animal-bondage. I've had extraordinarily good luck in maneuvering through physically threatening and dangerous situations involving peoples' animalistic behavior, and have generally not been caught up in things like territoriality or baseness. What's more, is that my attention is freed up from these things. Spiritually speaking, I have yet to understand the more exact effects of vegetarianism. I would suspect it would inhibit the capacity to manifest concrete happenings, at the same time, it makes one less interested in and vested in the material.
